Facts.text: King Alfonso III of Aragon invades Minorca
Pope Gregory XI moves the Papacy back to Rome from Avignon
Cesare Borgia returns in triumph to Rome from Romagna
Francois Rabelais absolved of apostasy by Pope Paul III
France gains Bresse, Bugey, Valromey & Gex in treaty with Spain
Frederick William I of Brandenburg and King Charles X Gustav of Sweden sign the Treaty of Königsberg
Avalanche destroys every building in Leukerbad, Switzerland; kills 53
Battle of Falkirk Muir, the Jacobites under Charles Stuart defeat Hanoverian forces
German Diet declares war on Prussia
9 old women burnt as witches for causing bad harvests in Kalisk, Poland
Richard Brinsley Sheridan's play "The Rivals" premieres in London
CaptainJames Cook's last notation in Discovery's ship's log

Battle of Cowpens: American revolutionary forces led by Brigadier General Daniel Morgan defeat the British under Lieutenant Colonel Banastre Tarleton in a decisive double envelopment near the town of Cowpens, a turning point in the American reconquest of South Carolina
Maltese patriot Dun Mikiel Xerri, along with a number of other patriots, is executed
Johannes van den Bosch appointed governor general of Dutch East Indies
British recognize independence of Transvaal (in South Africa)
-Jan 22nd) BBT Ft Henry, Tennessee by USS Lexington
Civil War skirmish near Newtown, Virginia
GeneralJames Longstreet's command ends heavy fighting at Dandridgem, Tennessee
1st cable car patented, by Andrew Smith Hallidie in the US (begins service in 1873)
Modoc warriors defeats the United States Army in the First Battle of the Stronghold, a part of the Modoc War
Armed Democrats seize Texas government ending Radical Reconstruction
1st Dutch female physician Aletta Jacobs opens her office
Battle of Abu Klea (Battle of Abu Tulayh), Sudan: British Desert Column defeat Mahdist forces: 76 British and 1,100 Mahdists killed
-17°F (-27°C), Millsboro, Delaware (state record)
Franco-Russian Alliance is signed
Queen Liliuokalani is deposed by an American coup, Kingdom of Hawaii becomes a republic
French president Casimir-Perier resigns; Félix Faure installed.
US takes possession of Wake Island in the Pacific
Punchboards patented by Charles Brewer & C G Scannell, Chicago
Percy Mackaye's "Scarecrow" premieres in NYC
Antoni vanLeeuwenhoek's Hospital in Amsterdam opens
Russia occupies Bukovina & Western Ukraine
Rodman Wanamaker organises a lunch to discuss forming a golfers association (later the PGA) at the Taplow Club, Martinique Hotel, New York City
US pays Denmark $25 million for Virgin Islands (or 3/21)
First day of prohibition of alcohol comes into effect in the US as a result of the 18th Amendment to the Constitution
Paul Deschanel elected President of France
Belgian Working people Party protest against occupied Ruhrgebied
Origin of Brown lunation numbers
1st fully automatic photographic film developing machine patented
Popeye makes 1st appearance, in comic strip "Thimble Theater"
Electric Home & Farm Authority incorporated
King Vidor is elected as first president of the newly formed Screen Directors Guild in Hollywood
Supreme Soviet elects Michail Kalinin as presidium chairman
Ed Barrow is elected Yankee president succeeding deceased J Ruppert
Tin Can Drive Day in the US, salvage collected for the war effort
British corvette HMS Violet sinks U-641 in Atlantic Ocean
Gilbert Dodds, record miler (4:05.3), retires to do gospel work
Soviet army enters the devastated city of Warsaw and clears German resistance
Swedish diplomat Raoul Wallenberg, credited with saving tens of thousands of Jews from the Nazis, arrested by Soviet secret police in Hungary
United Nations Security Council holds its 1st meeting, at Westminster Central Hall in Westminster, England
Ammunition factory explodes in Muiden, Netherlands, 16 die
Netherlands & Indonesia agree to a cease-fire
Trial of 11 US Communist party members begins in NYC
Radio series "The Goldbergs" by Gertrude Berg premieres on US TV on CBS
The Great Brinks Robbery - 11 men rob $1.2M cash & $1.5M securities from armored car company Brink's offices in Boston, Massachusetts
China refuses ceases-fire in Korea
1952 NFL Draft: Bill Wade QB University of Vanderbilt #1 pick by Los Angeles Rams
US Submarine Nautilus begins 1st nuclear-powered test voyage
9-county commission recommends creation of BART
Roy Harris' 8th Symphony, premieres in San Francisco
Joe Walker takes X-15 to altitude of 82 km
B-52/KC-135 tankers crash near Spanish coast at Palomares, 7 die
Soviet spacecraft Soyuz 5 lands
357 baseball players are available in the free-agent draft
John M Burgess installed as bishop of Protestant Episcopals (Mass)
At a party conference in Dublin, Sinn Féin end their 65 year abstentionist policy and agree that any elected representative could take their seat at the Dáil
Super Bowl V, Miami Orange Bowl, Miami, FL: Baltimore Colts beat Dallas Cowboys, 16-13; MVP: Chuck Howley, Dallas, LB
"My Hang-Up Is You" single released by Freddie Hart (Billboard Song of the Year 1972)
Section of Memphis' Highway 51 South renamed Elvis Presley Boulevard
Seven men who were held as internees escape from the prison ship HMS Maidstone in Belfast Lough, North Ireland
City of Amsterdam decides to support Hanoi
New Philippine constitution names Marcos president for life
Hermes rocket launched by European Space Agency
Gary Gilmore is executed by firing squad in the Utah state prison, convicted of murder
KC releases Tommy Davis, ends an 18-year career with 10 teams
Zaire president Mobutu visits Belgium
USSR performs underground nuclear test
NASA launches Fltsatcom-3
Provisional Irish Republican Army bomb prematurely detonates on a passenger train near Belfast, kills three and injuring five (including the bombers)
"Cold Sunday" in the United States would see temperatures fall to their lowest levels in over 100 years in numerous cities
Nigeria expels 2 million illegal aliens, mostly Ghanaians
Supreme Court rules (5-4) that private use of home VCRs to tape TV programs for later viewing does not violate federal copyright laws
Azharuddin scores second Test century in second Test (v England)
Tim Witherspoon beats defending champion Tony Tubbs by majority decision in 15 rounds at The Omni, Atlanta for WBA heavyweight boxing title
Leslie Manigay elected President of Haiti
Gunman opens fire in California schoolyard; 5 students slain, 30 wounded
Murden & Metz are 1st women to reach South Pole overland (on skis)
Phoenix Suns cancel game at Miami Heat, due to racial unrest in Miami
MLB pitcher Dave Stewart signs record $3,500,000 per year Oakland A's contract
Andy Van Hellemond sets NHL record for most games worked by a referee when he appears in his 1,173rd regular season encounter as the St. Louis Blues host the Montreal Canadiens
Harald V becomes King of Norway on the death of his father, Olav V
Iraq fires 8 Scud missiles on Israel
Operation Desert Storm begins, with US-led coalition forces bombing Iraq, during the Gulf War
Sarah Ferguson attends dinner of Everglades club (club excludes Jews)
6.6 Earthquake hits Los Angeles killing 60, $30B in damage
7.2 earthquake destroys Kobe, Japan and kills 5,372
NFL Los Angeles Rams announce that they are moving to St. Louis, Missouri
Iraq agrees to talks concerning a UN plan to allow for the Iraqi sale of $1 billion of oil; proceeds from the sale would be used for humanitarian purposes
The Czech Republic applies for membership of the European Union.
OPEC agrees at a meeting of ministers in Vienna, to reduce members' oil production quotas by 1.5 million barrels per day
Mount Nyiragongo erupts in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, displacing an estimated 400,000 people.
Doomsday Clock set to five minutes to midnight in response to North Korea's 1st nuclear test
A dragon boat from Chung Ling High School capsizes and collides with a tugboat amid strong currents in Penang, Malaysia, killing 6 people and injuring 12
"Sure Thing" single released by Miguel (Billboard Song of the Year 2011)
106 people are massacred by Syrian army forces in Homs
33 people are killed by a series of bombs across Iraq
4 people are killed and over 2,000 are evacuated after a massive flood hits Jakarta, Indonesia
8 people are killed in a plane crash in Chiapas, Mexico
Japan unveils plans to build the world’s largest wind farm near the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant
21 people are killed in a suicide bombing in a restaurant in Kabul, Afghanistan

American boxer Deontay Wilder wins the WBC heavyweight championship by unanimous decision over Bermane Stiverne at MGM Grand Garden Arena, Las Vegas; Wilder first American to hold a heavyweight title since Shannon Briggs in 2007
Nigerian military mistakenly bomb refugee camp killing more than 70 in Rann, Borno state
Car bomb in Bogotá, Colombia, outside the National Police Academy kills at least 8 and injures 41
China executes Cai Dongjia, the country's "godfather of crystal meth" from Boshe, Guangdong
Indian spiritual leader Ram Rahim Singh and two aides sentenced to life in prison for murdering journalist who exposed sexual abuse at the sect
Russian opposition leader Alexey Navalny is arrested immediately on his return to Russia after recovering from nerve-agent poisoning
Revival of 1980s American TV sitcom "Night Court", premieres on NBC TV; John Larrquette is the sole returning cast member
In a major victory, Fifth Circuit of the U.S. Court of Appeals upholds a lower court decision to block key provisions of HB 900, Texas’s controversial book rating law
Earliest evidence for a human transport vehicle uncovered, with drag marks made by travois - long pieces of wood loaded with goods and dragged, 22,000 years old at White Sands, New Mexico
Pittsburgh Penguins Alex Nedeljkovic becomes first goalie in NHL history to have a goal and an assist in same game during 5-2 road win over the Buffalo Sabres
Italian explorerGiovanni da Verrazzano's sets sail aboard French ship La Dauphine to find a passage to China - finds North America instead
French regentCatherine de' Mediciissues the Edict of St Germain recognizing Huguenots in France
KingHenry IV of Francedeclares war on Spain - lasts till 1598
CaptainJames Cookbecomes the first to cross Antarctic Circle (66° 33' S)
Mexico permitsMoses Austinand 300 US families to settle in Texas. After his death his son Stephen leads a colony to Texas.
Duke of Wellingtonappointed British supreme commander
Anton Chekhov's play "Cherry Orchard" opens at Moscow Art Theater
Failed assassination attempt on French Prime MinisterAristide Briandin the French Assembly
Raymond Poincaréelected president of France
Gerhart Hauptmann's play "Der Bogen des Odysseus" premieres in Berlin
Battle of Sarikamish ends with a Russian victory over Ottoman forces led byEnver Pashawith loss of over 30,000 men of the Turkish Third Army during Caucasus Campaign
NY Giants reward NL MVP pitcherCarl Hubbellwith a huge $18,000 contract
Joseph P. Kennedybecomes the 44th United States Ambassador to the United Kingdom
Chiang Kai-Shekdisbands the Communist New Fourth Army after the New Fourth Army incident at Maolin, Anhui Province between the nationalist and communist armies leaves many dead
Nazis begin evacuation ofAuschwitzconcentration camp to other camps in Austria and Germany as the Red Army advanced across Poland
Jacques Cousteau's 1st network telecast airs on "Omnibus" (CBS)

US PresidentDwight D. Eisenhowerdelivers a televised farewell address to the nation three days before leaving office, in which he warns against the accumulation of power by the "military-industrial complex".
NASA civilian pilotNeil Armstrongtakes hypersonic research plane  X-15 to 40,690m
Martin Luther King Jr.opens the Chicago Campaign, a nonviolent campaign aimed at achieving fair housing practices
Al Pacinostars in "The Indian Wants the Bronx" at the Astor Place Theatre, winning an Obie Award for Best Actor
Sporting News namesWillie Maysas Player of the Decade for the 1960s
Asylum Records releases "Court and Spark", Canadian singer-songwriterJoni Mitchell's 6th studio album; features the songs "Help Me" and "Free Man In Paris", it becomes her biggest seller
"I Write the Songs" cover byBarry Manilowhits #1
Blondie release their debut single "X Offender" written byDebbie Harryand Gary Valentine
Roger Sessions' 9th Symphony, his last, premieres in Syracuse, New York with Christopher Keene conducting the Syracuse Symphony Orchestra
President of the PhilippinesFerdinand Marcosends martial law, that began in September 1972
Czech tennis starIvan Lendlscores a 6–7, 2–6, 7–6, 6–2, 6–4 win over AmericanVitas Gerulaitisto claim his first of 5 ATP Masters Grand Prix titles at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Alabama GovernorGeorge Wallace, becomes governor for a record 4th time
General Secretary of the Chinese Communist PartyHu Yaobangis forced to resign, amid demonstrations for greater democracy
USPresident Reagansigns secret order permitting covert sale of arms to Iran
Al Arbourwins his 600th NHL game as coach

5th Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees: Hank Ballard; Bobby Darin; The Four Seasons; The Four Tops; The Kinks; The Platters; Simon and Garfunkel; The Who;Louis Armstrong; Charlie Christian; Ma Rainey; Gerry Goffin andCarole King; and Brian Holland, Lamont Dozier and Eddie Holland
ActressElizabeth Taylorreleased from the hospital after hip treatment
Patrick Ewingbecomes first New York player to reach the 15,000-point mark in his NBA career; scores a game-high 34 points in the Knicks' 106-94 win over visiting Minnesota
George W. Bushsworn in as Governor of Texas
11th Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees:David Bowie; Jefferson Airplane; Little Willie John;Gladys Knightand the Pips;Pink Floyd; The Shirelles; The Velvet Underground;Pete Seeger; and Tom Donahue
Detroit centerSteve Yzermanscores his 500th career NHL goal as the Red Wings beat Colorado, 3-2 at Joe Louis Arena and joins Gordie Howe as the only players to score 500 goals for Detroit
NBA suspendsDennis Rodmanindefinitely for kicking cameraman
US PresidentBill Clintonfaces sexual harassment charges from Paula Jones

President Clinton honorsLewis and Clark Expedition, posthumously raisesWilliam Clark's rank from Lieutenant to Captain, makes Honorary Sergeant Shoshone female guideSacagawea, and York, enslaved by William Clark, who assisted expedition

LeBron Jamesbecomes youngest player in NBA history to record 20,000 career points during the Miami Heat's 92-75 victory over Golden State; James, 28 years, 17 days, passes Kobe Bryant, 29 years, 122 days
Fourth Democratic presidential candidates debate:Hillary ClintonandBernie Sandersclash over healthcare and gun control in Charleston, South Carolina
US PresidentBarack Obamacommutes WikiLeaks discloser Chelsea Manning's prison sentence from 35 to 7 years
UK royal palace announcesCatherine, Princess of Walesis hospitalized for abdominal surgery and KingCharles IIIis receiving prostate treatment
Bohemia adopts Gregorian calendar
CaptainRobert Scott's expedition arrives at the South Pole, one month after Roald Amundsen
After a tumultuous final tourthe Sex Pistolsbreak up as a band, which is announced the following day
Search for missing aircraft MH370 over the Indian Ocean is called off
Philip the Bold, Duke of Burgundy (1363-1404), born in Pontoise, France (d. 1404)
Frederick III [Frederick the Wise], Elector of Saxony (1486-25) who protected his subject Martin Luther, born in Torgau, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1525)
George Spalatin, German humanist, reformer and important figure in the Reformation, born in Spalt, Bavaria (d. 1545)
Leonhard Fuchs, German physician and botanist (Notable Commentaries on the History of Plants) for which the flowering plants and color Fuchsia is named after, born in Wemding, Bavaria (d. 1566)
Antonio Scandello, Italian composer (Passion of John), born in Bergamo, Republic of Venice (d. 1580)
Antonio Pace, Italian madrigal composer and music publisher, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1581)
Gaspard Bauhin, Swiss botanist who published  some of the first works of systemic botany, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1624)
Robert Fludd, British physician, author, and mystical philosopher, baptized in Bearsted, Kent, England (d. 1637)
Pedro Calderón de la Barca, Spanish dramatist, poet and writer of the Spanish Golden Age, born in Madrid (d. 1681)
Pieter van Bloemen [Standaart], Flemish landscape painter, baptized in Antwerp (d. 1720)
Antonio Veracini, Italian violinist and Baroque composer, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1733)
Gerard van Loon, Dutch lawyer, numismatist and writer (Contemporary Numismatics, multi-volume works on Netherlands medals and obsidional coinage), born in Delft, Dutch Republic (d. 1758)[1]
Archibald Bower, Scottish historian who changed religion three times, known for "History of the Popes", born in Dundee, Scotland (d. 1766)
John Stanley, English blind composer and organist, born in London, England (d. 1786)
Johann Elias Schlegel, German critic and dramatic poet, born in Meissen, Germany (d. 1749)
William Vernon, American merchant in the slave trade and outfitter of the Continental navy, born in Newport, Colony of Rhode Island (d. 1806)
Jean-Joseph Vadé, French songwriter, poet, and playwright, born in Ham, France (d. 1757)
Hugh Mercer, Scottish military officer (fought in the Continental Army during the American Revolutionary War), born in Rosehearty, Scotland (d. 1777)
Johann Gottfried Müthel, German keyboard virtuoso and composer, born in Mölln, Duchy of Lauenburg (d. 1788)
Stanislaw II August Poniatowski, last King of Poland (1764-95), born in Wołczyn, Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth (d. 1795)
Thomas Linley the elder, English composer and tenor, born in Badminton, Gloucestershire, England (d. 1795)
François-Joseph Gossec, Belgian-French composer (Les Pêcheurs; Mirza), born in Vergnies, Austrian Netherlands (d. 1829)
Nicolas Roze, French composer, born in Mercurey, France (d. 1819)
Paul Cuffe, African American civil rights activist, merchant and shipbuilder (helped settle Sierra Leone), born on Cuttyhunk Island, Massachusetts (d. 1817)
James Hall, Scottish geologist, born in Dunglass, Scotland (d. 1832)
Ole Andreas Lindeman, Norwegian organist (Trondheim Vor Frelsers Kirke, 1799-57), composer, and educator, born in Øye, Surnadal, Norway (d. 1857)
Charles Brockden Brown 'Father of American novel', American pioneering writer and editor (Wieland), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1810)
August Neander [David Mendel], German theologian, born in Göttingen (d. 1850)
James Madison Randolph, American President Jefferson's grandson and the 1st to be born in the White House, born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1834)
Ellen Wood, English author (East Lynne, Pomeroy Abbey), born in Worcester, England (d. 1887)
Antoine-Aimé Dorion, Canadian politician and jurist (joint premier of Canada 1858, 1863-64), born in Ste-Anne-de-la-Pérade, Lower Canada (d. 1891)
Anne Brontë, English novelist/poet (Tenant of Wildfell Hall), born in Thornton, West Yorkshire (d. 1849)
Eduard Reményi, Hungarian violinist and composer, born in Miskolc, Austria-Hungary (d. 1898) [some sources cite birth year as 1830]
Lewis Addison Grant, American teacher, lawyer, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Winhall, Vermont (d. 1918)
Eyre Massey Shaw, British first chief of London Fire Brigade, born in Ballymore, Ireland (d. 1908)
Elisabeth Franziska of Austria, Austrian Archduchess, born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1903)
August Weismann, German biologist, one of the founders of the science of genetics, born in Frankfurt  (d. 1914)
Wilhelmina Albregt-Engelman, Dutch actress, daughter of Mimi Bia, born in Amsterdam (d. 1902)
Antanas Baranauskas, Lithuanian bishop, poet (Anykščių šilelis) and dialectologist, born in Anykščiai, Vilnius Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1902)
Johan Filip von Schantz, Finnish violinist, conductor, and composer (Kullervo), born in Ulvila, Grand Duchy of Finland, Russian Empire (d. 1865)
Jose White Lafitte, Cuban-French violinist and composer, born in Matanzas, Cuba (d. 1918)
Alexander Sergeyevich Taneyev, Russian composer, born in St Petersburg, Russia (d. 1918)
A. B. Frost, American illustrator known for Brer Rabbit, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1928)
Eugene Augustin Lauste, French inventor (1st sound-on-film recording), born in Montmartre, France (d. 1935)
Wilhelm Kienzl, Austrian composer (Der Evangelimann - The Evangelist; Der Kuhreigen), born in Waizenkirchen, Austria (d. 1941)
Tomas Carrasquilla, Colombian author (Frutos de mi Tierra), born in Santo Domingo, Colombia (d. 1940)
Douglas Hyde, Irish politician, President of Ireland (1938-45), co-founder of the Gaelic League, born in Castlerea, Ireland (d. 1949)
John Evert Akkeringa, Dutch painter and etcher, born in Blinjoe, Dutch East Indies (d. 1942)
Henry Charles Tonking, British organist and composer, born in Camborne, Cornwall, England (d. 1926)
Konstantin Stanislavski, Russian director and actor (Stanislavski Method for acting), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1938)
Lucien Herr, French intellectual and librarian (École Normale Supérieure), born in Altkirch, Haut-Rhin, France (d. 1926)
Sir Charles Fergusson, 7th Baronet, British army officer and Governor-General of New Zealand (1924-30), born in London (d. 1951)
Carl Laemmle, German-American film executive and founder of Universal Pictures, born in Laupheim, Kingdom of Württemberg (d. 1939)
Florencio Sánchez, Uruguayan dramatist, born in Montevideo, Uruguay (d. 1910)
Frank Hague, American politician, Mayor of Jersey City (1917-47), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 1956)
Olga Fastrová, Czech writer and editor (National Policy), born in Prague (d. 1965)
Hans Jelmoli, Swiss pianist and composer, born in Zurich, Switzerland (d. 1936)
May Gibbs, Australian children's author known for "Snugglepot and Cuddlepie", born in Sydenham, England (d. 1969)
Oscar Apfel, American actor (The Squaw Man, Inspiration, Before Dawn), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1938)
Alfred Radcliffe-Browne, British anthropologist (Andaman Islanders), born in Birmingham, England (d. 1955)
Antoni Łomnicki, Polish mathematician murdered by the Nazis during the massacre of the Lwów professors, born in Lwów, Poland (d. 1941)
Noah Beery, American character actor (Story of Esther, The Mark of Zorro), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1946)
Emmy Hennings-Ball [Emma Cordsen], German poet and performer, born in Flensburg, German Empire (d. 1948)
Glenn Luther Martin, American pioneering aviator (founded what became Lockheed Martin)), born in Macksburg, Iowa (d. 1955)
Ronald Firbank, British novelist (The Flower Beneath the Foot), born in London, England (d. 1926)
Ola Raknes, Norwegian psychoanalyst and philologist, born in Bergen, Norway (d. 1975)
Louis Santop, American Baseball HOF catcher (5 x NgL East All-Star; Hilldale Athletic Club), born in Tyler, Texas (d. 1942)
Marjorie Gateson, American actress (One Man's Family), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1977)
Walter Eucken, German economist (social market economy), born in Jena in Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach (d. 1950)
Harry Reser, American banjo player and bandleader (Sammy Kaye Show), born in Piqua, Ohio (d. 1965)
Marcel Petiot, French serial killer known as 'Doctor Satan' who murdered Jews for money during WWII, born in Auxerre, France (d. 1946)
Nils Asther, Swedish actor (Bluebeard, Night Monster), born in Malmö, Sweden (d. 1981)
Nevil Shute [Norway], English-Australian novelist (On the Beach, Town Like Alice), born in London, England (d. 1960)
Robert Maynard Hutchins, American educational philosopher (University of Chicago), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1977)
Roel Houwink, Dutch writer and poet (1 Man Without Character), born in Breda (d. 1987)
George Sperti, American inventor (Preparation H), born in Covington, Kentucky (d. 1991)
Olga Maria Nicolis di Robilant, Portuguese patron of the arts, born in Turin, Italy (d. 1996)
Aron Gurwitsch, Lithuanian-born philosopher, born in Vilnius, Lithuania (d. 1973)
Olin Dutra, American golfer (PGA C'ship 1932, US Open 1934), born in Monterey, California (d. 1983)
Vasily Petrovich Shirinsky, Russian violinist and composer, born in Ekaterinodar, Russia (d. 1965)
Jyoti Prasad Agarwala, Indian poet, playwright, filmmaker who made the first Assam film "Joymoti", born in Dibrugarh District, Assam (d. 1953)
Warren Hull, American actor (Strike it Rich, Who in the World), born in Gasport, New York (d. 1974)
Patsy Ruth Miller, American actress (Quebec, Wide Open, Sap, Twin Beds), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1995)
Eduard Oja, Estonian composer (Trilogy of Time), conductor, music teacher and violinist, born in Palupõhja, Livonia, Russian Empire (d. 1950)
Franz Schmid, German climber (1st to ascend northside of Matterhorn 1931), born in Garmisch, Germany (d. 1992)
Grant Withers, American actor (Rio Grande, Oklahoma, Annie), born in Pueblo, Colorado (d. 1959)
Peggy Gilbert [Margaret Knechtges], American jazz saxophonist, pianist, and bandleader (Metro Goldwyn Orchestra; Symphonics; Dixie Belles), born in Sioux City, Iowa (d. 2007)[1]
Henk Badings, Dutch opera composer (Orestes), born in Bandung, Java, Dutch East Indies (d. 1987)
Bryan Valentine, British cricket batsman (7 Tests, 2 x 100; Kent CCC, Cambridge University CC), born in Blackheath, England (d. 1983)
L. V. Prasad, Indian pioneering filmmaker, born in Somavarappadu, Madras Presidency, British India (d. 1994)
Karen Jønsson (née Pedersen), Danish jazz singer, pianist, songwriter, composer, and actress, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1942)
Edith Green, American politician (Rep-D-Oregon 1955-74), born in Trent, South Dakota (d. 1987)
Sidney “Big Sid” Catlett, American jazz drummer (Louis Armstrong), born in Evansville, Indiana (d. 1951)
Hermann Pfrogner, Austrian musicologist (Zerrissene Orpheus), and educator (Munich Music College, 1958-74), born in Graz, Austria-Hungary (d. 1988)
Orest Evlahkov, Russian Soviet composer and educator (Leningrad Conservatory), born in Warsaw, Russian Poland (d. 1973)
Werenfried van Straaten, Dutch anti-communist priest (founder of Oostpriesterhulp), born in Mijdrecht, Netherlands (d. 2003)
Yadavendra Singh [Yuvraj of Patiala], Indian 9th and last ruling Maharaja of Patiala and cricketer (scored 24 & 60 in only Test India v England), born in Patiala City, British Raj (d. 1974)
Anacleto Angelini, Italian-Chilean businessman (AntarChile), South America's richest man worth $6 billion, born in Ferrara, Italy (d. 2007)
Joel Herron, American orchestra leader (Jaye P Morgan Show), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2012)
M. G. Ramachandran, Indian actor and politician, born in Kandy, Sri Lanka (d. 1987)
Oskar Morawetz, Czech-Canadian composer (From the Diary of Anne Frank), born in Světlá nad Sázavou, Bohemia (d. 2007)
Ramón Cardemil, Chilean huaso, born in Colchagua, Chile (d. 2007)
Joseph W. Barr, American banker and politician (Secretary of the Treasury), born in Vincennes, Indiana (d. 1996)
Keith Joseph, British Conservative politician and minister under four PMs who helped create the ideology of Thatcherism, born in London (d. 1994)
George Handy [Hendleman], American bebop jazz arranger, pianist and composer (Handyland, U.S.A.), born in New York City (d. 1997)
Nora Kaye, American prima ballerina, born in New York City (d. 1987)
Antonio Prohias, Cuban-American cartoonist for "Mad" magazine (Spy vs. Spy series), born in Cienfuegos, Cuba (d. 1998)
Dehl Berti, Chiricahua Apache actor (Bonanza, Gunsmoke, Guns of Paradise), born in Pueblo, Colorado (d. 1991)
Luis Echeverria Alvarez, Mexican politician, President of Mexico (1970-76), born in Mexico City (d. 2022)
Nicholas Katzenbach, lawyer (US Attorney General), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2012)
Carol Raye [Kathleen Corkery], British-born Australian actress (Number 96; Blankety Blanks), born in London (d. 2022)
Onno Molenkamp, Dutch actor (Lifespan, Broken Mirrors, The Lift), born in Fort de Kock, Dutch East Indies (d. 1990)
Jewel Plummer Cobb, American biologist and cancer researcher (melanoma), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2017)
Abdul Hafeez Kardar, Pakistani cricketer and politician (captained Pakistan's 1st Test, prev played for India), born in Lahore, British India (d. 1996)
Annie Delorie, Dutch opera singer (Nederlandse Opera, 1950-70), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2009)
Duane Hanson, American artist, made life-sized realistic sculptures of people, born in Alexandria, Minnesota (d. 1996)
Edgar Ray Killen, American convict, former preacher, K.K.K. member and murderer, born in Philadelphia, Mississippi (d. 2018)
Robert Cormier, American author known for "The Chocolate War" and "I Am the Cheese", born in Leominster, Massachusetts (d. 2000)
Dick Hills, British screenwriter, with Sid Green (Two Of A Kind; The Strange World of Gurney Slade), and comedian (Those Two Fellers), born in Eltham, England  (d. 1996)
Moira Shearer, Scottish actress and ballerina (Red Shoes), born in Dunfermline Scotland (d. 2006)
Newton N. Minow, American lawyer, statesman (FCC Chairman, 1961-63), and public broadcasting advocate, born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 2023)
Sir Clyde Walcott, West Indian cricket batsman (44 Tests @ 56.68; 15 x 100s) and administrator (ICC chairman 1997-2000), born in St Michael, Barbados (d. 2006)
Donald Erb, American composer known for large orchestral works, born in Youngstown, Ohio (d. 2008)
Eartha Kitt, American singer (Santa Baby), dancer, activist, and actress (Batman - "Catwoman"), born in North, South Carolina (d. 2008)
Michael Wooller, British TV and film producer (Omnibus), born in Lancashire, England (d. 1996)
Thomas Anthony Dooley, American humanitarian in Vietnam and Laos, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1961)
Jean Barraqué, French composer, born in Hauts-de-Seine, France (d. 1973)
Ken Archer, Australian cricket batsman (5 Tests; Queensland CA), born in Brisbane, Australia (d. 2023)
Philip Jones, British classical trumpeter (Philip Jones Brass Ensemble, 1951-86), and educator, born in Bath, England (d. 2000)
